quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Enthalpy (H)"?

options.text: An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility
A thermodynamic state function equal to internal energy plus PV; ΔH = heat exchanged at constant pressure
Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area
Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Enthalpy (H)" is defined as: A thermodynamic state function equal to internal energy plus PV; ΔH = heat exchanged at constant pressure. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Calorimetry"?

options.text: The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)
The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve
The experimental measurement of heat changes using an insulated container; q = mcΔT for solution calorimetry
A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Calorimetry" is defined as: The experimental measurement of heat changes using an insulated container; q = mcΔT for solution calorimetry. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)"?

options.text: The enthalpy change for forming 1 mole of compound from elements in standard states (25°C, 1 atm); ΔH°f = 0 for elements
(Mass of desired product / Total mass of all products) × 100%; measures efficiency of atom utilization in a reaction
The arrangement of only the atoms (bonding pairs) around the central atom (e.g., bent for 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs)
A tube filled with inert electrolyte (KNO₃, KCl) connecting two half-cells; allows ion migration to maintain electrical neutrality

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)" is defined as: The enthalpy change for forming 1 mole of compound from elements in standard states (25°C, 1 atm); ΔH°f = 0 for elements. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "State function"?

options.text: Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3; each successive proton is harder to remove because it leaves a more negatively charged anion
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water
A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
A property that depends only on the current state (T, P, composition), not on the path taken; examples: H, S, G, E

quiz_questions.explanation: "State function" is defined as: A property that depends only on the current state (T, P, composition), not on the path taken; examples: H, S, G, E. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Bomb calorimeter"?

options.text: A sealed, constant-volume calorimeter for measuring heat of combustion: q = CcalΔT, where Ccal is the calorimeter constant
A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products
Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system
Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area

quiz_questions.explanation: "Bomb calorimeter" is defined as: A sealed, constant-volume calorimeter for measuring heat of combustion: q = CcalΔT, where Ccal is the calorimeter constant. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A thermodynamic state function equal to internal energy plus PV; ΔH = heat exchanged at constant pressure"?

options.text: Ka (acid dissociation constant)
Buffer capacity
Integrated rate law
Enthalpy (H)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Enthalpy (H)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The experimental measurement of heat changes using an insulated container; q = mcΔT for solution calorimetry"?

options.text: Lewis structure
Alloy
Calorimetry
Standard reduction potential (E°)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Calorimetry".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The enthalpy change for forming 1 mole of compound from elements in standard states (25°C, 1 atm); ΔH°f = 0 for elements"?

options.text: Lewis acid/base
Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)
Conjugate acid-base pair
Solubility product (Ksp)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A property that depends only on the current state (T, P, composition), not on the path taken; examples: H, S, G, E"?

options.text: Secondary battery
Rate law
Metallic bond
State function

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "State function".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A sealed, constant-volume calorimeter for measuring heat of combustion: q = CcalΔT, where Ccal is the calorimeter constant"?

options.text: Brønsted-Lowry base
Phase diagram
Bomb calorimeter
Conjugate acid-base pair

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Bomb calorimeter".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Enthalpy and Calorimetry is accurate?

options.text: If a reaction is reversed, ΔH changes sign; if multiplied by a factor n, ΔH is multiplied by n.
Equilibrium is dynamic (reactions continue but net change is zero).
Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
Enthalpy and Calorimetry measure the heat of reactions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Enthalpy and Calorimetry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Enthalpy and Calorimetry?

options.text: Comparing Qsp to Ksp predicts precipitation: if Qsp > Ksp, a precipitate forms.
Enthalpy (H) is a state function; ΔH = qp (heat at constant pressure).
Alloys (mixtures of metals) modify properties by introducing different-sized atoms.
Oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions involve electron transfer; oxidation is loss of electrons (OIL), reduction is gain (RIG).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Enthalpy and Calorimetry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Enthalpy and Calorimetry?

options.text: Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.
Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) and strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka or Kb values.
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.
Atomic radius decreases left to right (increasing Zeff) and increases top to bottom (more electron shells).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Enthalpy and Calorimetry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Enthalpy and Calorimetry?

options.text: Concentration expressions include molarity (M = mol/L), molality (m = mol/kg solvent), mole fraction, and mass percent.
Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f) is the enthalpy change when 1 mole of compound forms from elements in their standard states. ΔH°rxn = ΣΔH°f(products) - ΣΔH°f(reactants).
These trends explain reactivity patterns: alkali metals are highly reactive (low IE, lose e⁻ easily), while noble gases are inert (full valence shells, high IE).
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Enthalpy and Calorimetry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Enthalpy and Calorimetry?

options.text: The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.
Concentration expressions include molarity (M = mol/L), molality (m = mol/kg solvent), mole fraction, and mass percent.
Only a small fraction of collisions are effective.
Enthalpy and Calorimetry measure the heat of reactions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Enthalpy and Calorimetry as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Enthalpy and Calorimetry?

options.text: Antacid tablet surface area experiments: crushed tablets dissolve faster than whole tablets because increased surface area increases reaction rate
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Food calorie content measured by bomb calorimetry: burning food samples in pure O₂ and measuring the temperature rise of surrounding water
Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Enthalpy and Calorimetry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Enthalpy and Calorimetry?

options.text: Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment
Designing energy-efficient buildings using materials with known specific heat capacities and enthalpies for optimal thermal insulation
Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Steel alloys: adding carbon (interstitial) to iron increases hardness; adding chromium and nickel (substitutional) creates stainless steel resistant to corrosion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Enthalpy and Calorimetry.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Enthalpy and Calorimetry applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Swimming pool chemistry: maintaining pH 7.2-7.8 using acid (HCl) or base (NaHCO₃) additions to optimize chlorine disinfection effectiveness
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Enthalpy and Calorimetry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Enthalpy and Calorimetry?

options.text: Food calorie content measured by bomb calorimetry: burning food samples in pure O₂ and measuring the temperature rise of surrounding water
Chain reaction mechanisms in combustion: initiation produces radicals, propagation sustains the reaction, termination removes radicals (relevant to engine knocking and explosives)
The spontaneous rusting of iron: despite being exothermic (ΔH < 0), rust formation is also driven by the entropy increase from solid Fe converting to flaky Fe₂O₃
Fluorine's extreme electronegativity making it the most reactive nonmetal, used in Teflon (PTFE) coatings and uranium enrichment (UF₆)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Enthalpy and Calorimetry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Enthalpy and Calorimetry in practice?

options.text: Ocean buffering against acidification: carbonate/bicarbonate system absorbing excess CO₂, but capacity being overwhelmed by current emission rates
Gold's malleability allowing it to be hammered into gold leaf just 0.1 μm thick, used in electronics connections, architectural decoration, and aerospace heat shields
Designing energy-efficient buildings using materials with known specific heat capacities and enthalpies for optimal thermal insulation
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Enthalpy and Calorimetry.
